facilities.name: Hammond Water Works Filtration Plant
facilities.owner: City of Hammond, Indiana (Hammond Water Works Dept.)
facilities.note: 925 Casino Center Dr., Hammond, on the Lake Michigan shore a short distance east of the Illinois-Indiana state line. Current plant dates to the 1930s, but a Hammond municipal lake intake already existed by 1927 -- the Sanitary District's own sewage-disposal survey that year located the Whiting and Hammond, Indiana 'Water Works' intakes' between the Calumet River mouth and the Indiana Harbor Canal, warning that Chicago's Calumet-area sewage practices had to account for them. Today supplies roughly 300,000 people in NW Indiana and Chicago's SE suburbs, plus wholesale sales to Whiting (since 2010), Munster, Highland, Griffith, and Dyer. Coordinate geocoded (Nominatim) from the utility's Casino Center Dr. address; flagged approx pending confirmation this is the filtration/intake site rather than an office.
facilities.source: 1927 Sanitary District of Chicago (Alvord, Burdick & Howson), Disposal of Sewage, pp.103-105, local file sources/sanitary-district-annual-reports/1927-disposal-of-sewage-sd-chicago-alvord-burdick-howson.pdf; Hammond Water Works, About Us <https://hammondwaterworks.com/about-us-2/>; NWI Times reporting on BP Whiting outfall siting relative to the Hammond intake. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Whiting Water Works (historic, closed 2010)
facilities.note: Whiting began selling Lake Michigan water in 1903 and built its own filtration plant in 1920 (subject of a 1921 joint technical paper alongside Evanston, IL's plant). The city shut the plant down in June 2010 and has bought treated water from Hammond ever since. Precise historic plant-site coordinates were not confirmed in sources reviewed; placed near the Whiting lakefront/Whiting Park area adjoining the BP refinery and flagged approx.
facilities.source: NWI Times, "Whiting to demolish old water works" <https://nwitimes.com/news/local/lake/whiting-to-demolish-old-water-works/article_d26eae1f-c81d-53b8-9f2c-fe7ea2030d94.html>; City of Whiting, "Water" <https://www.whitingindiana.com/services/water_.php>; JSTOR, "Operation of the Water Filtration Plants at Evanston, Illinois, and Whiting, Indiana" (1921) <https://www.jstor.org/stable/41224799>. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: BP Whiting Refinery (industrial water intake/outfall)
facilities.owner: Standard Oil Co. of Indiana (1889-1985) / Amoco (1985-1998) / BP Products North America (1998-present)
facilities.note: Construction began May 1889 for Standard Oil of New Jersey; the Indiana subsidiary was incorporated the following month. By the mid-1890s it was the largest refinery in the U.S. (36,000 bbl/day, ~20% of national capacity); today, as BP's largest refinery worldwide, it processes 400,000+ bbl/day on the Lake Michigan shore. Draws lake water for cooling/process use and discharges roughly 18 MGD of treated wastewater through an outfall a few miles from Hammond's drinking-water intake -- a juxtaposition that has drawn IDEM/EPA scrutiny over mercury, PFAS, and other pollutants in its NPDES permit. Marker is a centroid of the ~1,700-acre complex, which straddles Whiting and Hammond; flagged approx.
facilities.source: American Oil & Gas Historical Society, "Standard Oil Whiting Refinery" <https://aoghs.org/products/standard-oil-whiting-refinery/>; Environmental Law & Policy Center, "Pollution Control Permit for BP Whiting Refinery..." <https://elpc.org/news/pollution-control-permit-for-bp-whiting-refinery-allows-plant-to-continue-dumping-mercury-and-forever-chemicals-into-lake-michigan/>; NWI Times, "BP Whiting Refinery discharged industrial waste into Lake Michigan." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: East Chicago Water Filtration Plant (historic Aldis Ave. site)
facilities.note: Historic plant on Aldis Ave. (this coordinate) built 1918, upgraded 1929 for industrial-era demand, rebuilt 1964. Replacement began construction Dec. 2009: a new membrane-filtration plant relocated south of Cline Ave. to free the lakeshore for other uses; the Aldis Ave. structure was later demolished. New plant's precise site and opening year not confirmed in sources reviewed (2013 used here as a rough placeholder). East Chicago sold water to Indiana American Water for part of the Gary area's supply from Aug. 1997 to Dec. 2016, when Indiana American switched fully to its own Borman Park (Gary) intake.
facilities.source: City of East Chicago, "Water Filtration Plant" <https://www.eastchicago.com/257/Water-Filtration-Plant>; NWI Times, "Indiana American customers received East Chicago water." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: State Line Generating Plant (historic, 1929-2012)
facilities.owner: Commonwealth Edison / Midwest Generation (Edison International); site redeveloped by Digital Crossroad
facilities.note: Coal-fired plant built 1926-1929 (Samuel Insull) directly on the Illinois-Indiana state line at the Lake Michigan shore in Hammond. Unit 1 (208 MW) was, on completion, the largest turbine generator built to that date and remained the largest of its kind in the world until 1954; capacity reached 515 MW by the plant's final configuration. Used Lake Michigan water for condenser cooling -- the site was chosen partly for lake access. Closed Mar. 31, 2012 (Hammond's largest taxpayer at the time); demolished over roughly two years; redeveloped 2018-2021 as the Digital Crossroad data-center campus.
facilities.source: Wikipedia, "State Line Generating Plant" <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_Line_Generating_Plant> (coordinates per infobox); Industrial Scenery, "1929-2012 ComEd State Line Generating Station" <http://industrialscenery.blogspot.com/2018/07/lostcomed-state-line-generating-station.html>; DatacenterDynamics, "Digital Crossroad finishes $40m Lake Michigan data center in Indiana." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: ComEd Calumet (100th Street) Generating Station (historic)
facilities.note: 100th St. & Commercial/Torrence Ave., South Chicago, directly on the Calumet River (built on a former ice pond per a 1915 map). Opened 1921. A 1965 U.S. HEW industrial-pollution survey found 'Commonwealth Edison Calumet Station' the single largest discharger among eight Calumet-area polluters, releasing 220 MGD of cooling water to the river -- more than all the steel mills in the same survey combined. No longer generates electricity and its exact closure year is undocumented in sources reviewed; ComEd retains the parcel as a switchyard/substation today. 'To' left null pending a documented closure date -- treat as ceased, not active.
facilities.source: Industrial Scenery, "Lost/ComEd Calumet (100th Street) Generation Station" <http://industrialscenery.blogspot.com/2018/06/lostcomed-calumet-generation-station.html>; Colten, Craig E., Industrial Wastes in the Calumet Area, 1869-1970, pp.63-66 (Table 4-2, 1965 HEW survey), local file sources/period-engineering-journals/1985-colten-industrial-wastes-calumet-area-1869-1970-historical-geography.pdf. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Fisk Generating Station (historic, 1903-2012)
facilities.note: 1111 W. Cermak Rd., Pilsen. In service Oct. 2, 1903, built by Chicago Edison Co. (Chairman Samuel Insull, Chief Engineer Frederick Sargent) directly on the South Branch of the Chicago River for both cooling water and coal-barge access. Condenser cooling water was drawn from and returned to the South Branch via an injection tunnel, cold well, and 36-in. circulating pump. A 2015 USGS study of Chicago Area Waterway System (CAWS) hydrology identified Fisk as one of three thermal power plants (with Crawford and Will County) then relying directly on CAWS flow volume for cooling, noting Fisk and Crawford had 'recently closed or [were] in the process of closing' as of 2014; Fisk in fact closed in 2012.
facilities.source: Wikipedia, "Fisk Generating Station" <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fisk_Generating_Station>; USGS, Scientific Investigations Report on CAWS hydrology and monitoring issues (2015), pp.51-52, local file sources/usgs-caws-hydrology/2015-usgs-sir-caws-hydrology-monitoring-issues.pdf; Nominatim geocode of 1111 W. Cermak Rd. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Crawford Generating Station (historic, 1924-2012)
facilities.note: 3501 S. Pulaski Rd., South Lawndale/Little Village, directly on the Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal. First generated 1924, officially online May 1925; designed by Graham, Anderson, Probst & White. Condenser cooling water was drawn from the CSSC and coal delivered by canal barge; by c.2010 two generators totaled 542 net MW. Closed Aug. 2012 after a long community campaign (Little Village Environmental Justice Organization) over air pollution; demolished 2019-2020. One of the two CAWS-dependent coal plants (with Fisk) the 2015 USGS hydrology study flagged as recently closed.
facilities.source: Library of Congress, HAER record, Commonwealth Edison Company, Crawford Electrical Generating Station, 3501 South Pulaski Road <https://www.loc.gov/item/il0708> (coordinates); Wikipedia, "Crawford Station"; Anderson & Meng, Municipal Wastewater Reuse in Metropolitan Chicago (2011 thesis), pp.27-28, local file sources/theses/2011-anderson-meng-municipal-wastewater-reuse-metropolitan-chicago.pdf; USGS SIR (2015) pp.51-52. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: CITGO Lemont Refinery
facilities.owner: CITGO Petroleum Corp. (PDVSA); predecessors Pure Oil (to 1965), Union Oil (1965-1993), UNO-VEN joint venture (1993-1997)
facilities.note: 135th St. & New Ave., Lemont/Romeoville, on the Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal at river mile 296.5, 5.5 mi upstream of the Lockport Lock & Dam. Original refinery built in the early 1920s (~25,000 bbl/day); rebuilt and expanded 1968-1970 by Union Oil to 135,000 bbl/day on 1,100 acres. CITGO assumed operation in 1997 when PDVSA bought out Unocal's share of the UNO-VEN partnership. Draws process and cooling water from, and discharges treated effluent back to, the Lower CSSC daily (design average 5.79 MGD at NPDES Outfall 001). 'From' year approximate (early-1920s founding date not pinned to a specific year in sources reviewed).
facilities.source: CITGO, "The Refinery - Lemont" <https://refining.stgweb.citgo.com/Lemont/About-Us>; Wikipedia, "Lemont Refinery"; H Engineering, Site Report, Citgo Petroleum Co., Lemont, IL <https://downloads.regulations.gov/EPA-HQ-OW-2018-0618-0292/content.pdf>. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: U.S. Steel South Works (historic, 1880-1992)
facilities.owner: North Chicago Rolling Mill / Illinois Steel Co. (to 1901) / U.S. Steel (1901-1992)
facilities.note: Between 79th and 87th St. at the Calumet River's mouth on Lake Michigan (redevelopment anchor point ~8080 S. DuSable Lake Shore Dr.). Operations moved here in 1882 for better lake/river anchorage for ore and coal boats and grew into the region's largest integrated mill; North Chicago Rolling Mill's slag dumping here from 1878-79 is separately documented in doc 15's Lake Calumet section. Closure announced Jan. 9, 1992; shut permanently Apr. 10, 1992 with fewer than 700 workers remaining. The 415-440-acre site is now in redevelopment (PsiQuantum quantum-computing campus groundbreaking, Oct. 2025).
facilities.source: Wikipedia, "South Works"; Chicago Sun-Times, "City's planning chief says South Works too big for one developer"; The Quantum Insider, "Chicago Breaks Ground on Quantum Campus at Former South Works Site" <https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/10/01/chicago-breaks-ground-on-quantum-campus-at-former-south-works-site/>; Nominatim geocode of "South Works, South Chicago, Chicago." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Wisconsin Steel Works (historic, 1875-1980)
facilities.owner: Joseph H. Brown Iron & Steel Co. (1875-1902) / International Harvester Co. (1902-1977) / Envirodyne Industries (1977-1980)
facilities.note: South Deering, on the Calumet River. Founded 1875 as Joseph H. Brown Iron & Steel; sold to International Harvester in 1902 to supply steel for farm equipment. A mid-1950s Corps of Engineers dredging-obstruction suit (with Republic Steel and Interlake Iron as co-defendants) found International Harvester's own sewer-system map showed 13.6 MGD of untreated waste plus 4.9 MGD of primary-treated waste entering the Calumet River daily, and the company was separately found to add 27,000+ tons/yr of solids to the channel; by the 1965 HEW survey the plant's permitted discharge was 40 MGD (flue dust, pickle liquor, coke breeze -- thickener/neutralizer treatment). Closed without notice Mar. 27, 1980, idling 3,300 workers.
facilities.source: Colten, Industrial Wastes in the Calumet Area, pp.51,58,63-66,74-77 (Tables 4-1 to 4-3), local file sources/period-engineering-journals/1985-colten-industrial-wastes-calumet-area-1869-1970-historical-geography.pdf; Chicago Public Library blog, "The Deindustrialization of Chicago's Southeast Side..."; Nominatim geocode "Wisconsin Steel (historic), South Deering, Chicago." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Interlake Iron / Acme Steel Coke Plant (historic, 1905-2001)
facilities.owner: By-Product Coke Corp. (1905-1929) / Interlake Iron Corp. (1929-1986) / Acme Steel Co. (1986-2001)
facilities.note: East bank of the Calumet River near S. Torrence Ave./114th St., across the river from Wisconsin Steel. Incorporated 1905 as By-Product Coke Corp.; renamed Interlake Iron Corp. 1929; sold to Acme Steel 1986. By the 1965 HEW survey the coke plant discharged 37 MGD of cooling/coke-quench water to the Calumet River (700 lb/day ammonia nitrogen, 900 lb/day phenolics, treated via thickeners and a closed coke quench). Ceased operating 2001. Listed to the Superfund National Priorities List as the 'Acme Steel Coke Plant' site (11236 S. Torrence Ave.) in March 2024 for cyanide, mercury, and SVOC contamination -- coordinate here is a Nominatim geocode of that Superfund address.
facilities.source: Colten, Industrial Wastes in the Calumet Area, pp.63-66 (Table 4-2/4-3), local file sources/period-engineering-journals/1985-colten-industrial-wastes-calumet-area-1869-1970-historical-geography.pdf; U.S. EPA, "Acme Steel Coke Plant" Superfund site profile <https://cumulis.epa.gov/supercpad/cursites/csitinfo.cfm?id=0509241>; Chicago Sun-Times, "Toxic Acme Steel site on Southeast Side becomes newest Chicago Superfund priority cleanup." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Republic Steel / LTV Steel, 114th St. Plant (historic, closed 2002)
facilities.note: East Side neighborhood, ~11600 S. Burley Ave., east bank of the Calumet River across from Wisconsin Steel/Interlake Iron. Precise founding year undocumented in sources reviewed (Republic Steel formed as a national holding company in 1930; local operations trace to the earlier Grand Crossing Tack Co. site) -- 1925 used here as a rough placeholder, flagged approx. Named with Wisconsin Steel and Interlake Iron as a co-defendant in the Corps of Engineers' mid-1950s dredging-obstruction suit (Republic's own figures showed 10,000+ tons/yr of solids entering the river from its outfalls, treated via thickeners and scale pits per the 1965 HEW survey). Under LTV ownership, discharges of 12-38 MGD of non-contact cooling water, groundwater, and stormwater to the Calumet River (Outfall 004) continued to closure in 2002.
facilities.source: Colten, Industrial Wastes in the Calumet Area, pp.63-66,74-77, local file sources/period-engineering-journals/1985-colten-industrial-wastes-calumet-area-1869-1970-historical-geography.pdf; Nominatim geocode "Old Republic Steel 114th Street Plant, South Burley Avenue, East Side, Chicago"; EPA site-characterization language re: LTV Outfall 004 (12-38 MGD). Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Youngstown Sheet & Tube / Iroquois Iron & Steel (historic, closed 1961/1960s)
facilities.owner: Iroquois Iron & Steel Co. (1890s-1923) / Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. (1923-1960s)
facilities.note: East (south) bank of the Calumet River at its mouth on Lake Michigan, across from South Works. Rogers Brown's Iroquois Iron & Steel operation began at 95th St. and the Calumet River in 1890, relocating to the river mouth around 1912; acquired by Youngstown Sheet & Tube in 1923 along with an East Chicago, IN plant. The South Chicago works was permanently shut down in Feb. 1961, though some Youngstown operations in the area continued into the late 1960s. Site later became the Illinois International Port District's Iroquois Landing terminal. Coordinate approximate (placed between South Works and the river mouth per source descriptions; no precise site address located).
facilities.source: Pullman State Historic Site / Southeast Historical Society image collection notes on Iroquois Iron & Steel; Encyclopedia of Chicago, "Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co."; Industrial Scenery, "Lighthouses and Steel Mills (YS&T/Iroquois and Illinois South Works) at Calumet River Mouth" <http://industrialscenery.blogspot.com/2018/06/lighthouses-and-youngstowniroquois.html>. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Indiana Harbor Steel Works (Inland/LTV/Youngstown -> ArcelorMittal -> Cleveland-Cliffs)
facilities.owner: Inland Steel Co. (1901-1998) / Ispat Inland-Mittal Steel (1998-2006) / ArcelorMittal (2006-2020) / Cleveland-Cliffs (2020-present)
facilities.note: East Chicago, IN, on Lake Michigan and the Indiana Harbor & Ship Canal, just east of the Illinois-Indiana state line and immediately outside this archive's mapped Illinois reach of the Grand Calumet River. Built on artificial landfill beginning 1901 by Inland Steel; today's ~3,100-acre complex (merging the former Inland, LTV, and Youngstown Indiana Harbor works) is North America's largest integrated steelmaking site, drawing large volumes of Lake Michigan water for process cooling -- e.g. the 80-in. hot strip mill quenches red-hot steel coil with an 'Olympic pool's worth' of lake water every few seconds. Included here, just outside the archive's usual mapped extent, as the dominant current industrial water user in the Calumet/Indiana Harbor system and the direct upstream/upwind neighbor of every Illinois-side facility in this file. Coordinate is a Nominatim centroid of the Indiana Harbor neighborhood, not a precise plant-building location; flagged approx.
facilities.source: The Beauty of Steel, "ArcelorMittal Indiana Harbor" <https://thebeautyofsteel.com/steel-plants-archive/arcelormittal-indianaharbor/>; A Chicago Sojourn, "Cold Water and Hot Steel" <https://achicagosojourn.wordpress.com/2014/11/09/cold-water-and-hot-steel/>; Center for Land Use Interpretation, "Indiana Harbor Works Steel Plant." Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: City of Elmhurst Wastewater Treatment Plant (non-MWRD)
facilities.owner: City of Elmhurst, Illinois (Public Works Dept., Utility Division)
facilities.note: 625 S. Route 83, Elmhurst -- Elmhurst lies in DuPage County outside MWRD's Cook County service area and has operated its own municipal wastewater utility rather than contracting to MWRD. Rated capacity 20 MGD, activated-sludge process, discharging directly to Salt Creek (within this archive's mapped Salt Creek reach). Construction/opening year not documented in sources reviewed. Coordinate is an approximate placement along Salt Creek near the documented street address (precise geocoding was unavailable via Nominatim); flagged approx.
facilities.source: City of Elmhurst, "Wastewater Collection & Treatment" <https://www.elmhurst.org/services/public_works/utility_division/wastewater_treatment/index.php> and business-detail listing <https://www.elmhurst.org/business_detail_T9_R10.php>. Accessed July 2026.

facilities.name: Salt Creek Sanitary District WWTP (non-MWRD, serves Villa Park)
facilities.note: 201 S. Route 83, Villa Park -- a second independent, non-MWRD district (distinct from and downstream-neighboring the City of Elmhurst's own plant) treating wastewater for Villa Park in DuPage County and discharging to Salt Creek. DuPage County's own government pages list it among the county's independent sanitary districts operating outside MWRD's Cook County jurisdiction. Construction/opening year not documented in sources reviewed. Coordinate approximate (precise geocoding unavailable); flagged approx.
facilities.source: Salt Creek Sanitary District, official site <https://www.saltcreeksd.com/>; DuPage County, "Salt Creek Sanitary District" <https://www.dupagecounty.gov/government/county_board/scsd.php>. Accessed July 2026.

connections.label: BP Whiting Refinery treated-wastewater outfall to Lake Michigan (~18 MGD)
connections.source: Environmental Law & Policy Center and NWI Times reporting on the BP Whiting NPDES outfall's proximity to the Hammond drinking-water intake. Approximate offshore endpoint, not a surveyed pipe route. Accessed July 2026.

connections.label: Hammond Water Works Lake Michigan intake
connections.source: Hammond Water Works, About Us <https://hammondwaterworks.com/about-us-2/>; general description of shoreline intake, precise crib/pipe location undocumented in sources reviewed. Accessed July 2026.

connections.label: CITGO Lemont Refinery NPDES Outfall 001 to the Lower Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal (river mile 296.5)
connections.source: H Engineering, Site Report, Citgo Petroleum Co., Lemont, IL <https://downloads.regulations.gov/EPA-HQ-OW-2018-0618-0292/content.pdf>; design average flow 5.79 MGD. Approximate waypoints, not a surveyed pipe route. Accessed July 2026.

connections.label: State Line Generating Plant Lake Michigan cooling-water intake (historic, 1929-2012)
connections.source: Wikipedia, "State Line Generating Plant"; Whiting-Robertsdale Historical Society, "The State Line Generating Station" <https://www.wrhistoricalsociety.com/the-state-line-generating-station>. Approximate offshore endpoint. Accessed July 2026.
